title: Moules marinières
subtitle: Mussels steamed in white wine, shallot, and parsley
excerpt: Moules marinières is mussels steamed open in white wine with shallots, garlic, and parsley. It takes fifteen minutes from start to table. The cooking liquid becomes the broth, and a good bread alongside is not optional.
sourceNotes:
• Auguste Escoffier, *Le Guide Culinaire* (1903, public domain). Reference for the classic moules marinières method — shallots, white wine, parsley, and the technique of steaming mussels in a covered pot rather than simmering them in liquid.
• The dish is a staple of Norman and Breton coastal cooking, with the Norman version using cream added at the end and the Breton version served without. This recipe follows the simpler Breton approach.

glossaryTerms.definition: In the manner of the mariner. In French cooking, à la marinière refers to seafood — usually mussels — cooked in white wine with shallots and herbs. The cooking liquid becomes the broth served with the dish.

Removing the byssus threads — the fibrous beard — from a mussel before cooking. Pull the beard firmly towards the hinge and it comes away. Not all mussels have a visible beard; farmed mussels are often sold already cleaned.

content.text: Mussels are fast, affordable, and deeply underused.

is the simplest version: white wine, shallot, garlic, parsley. The mussels steam open in the covered pot, the liquor they release mixes with the wine and shallot, and the result is a broth of considerable depth. The whole thing is ready in fifteen minutes.

The only preparation is cleaning: scrub the shells, pull the beards, and discard anything already open that doesn't close when tapped. Everything else is quick. Buy mussels on the day you intend to cook them and keep them cold.
Serve with crusty bread or frites. A large bowl per person and a separate bowl for the shells.
Tip {{mussels}} of mussels into a colander. Scrub each one under cold running water, scraping off any barnacles with a blunt knife.
each mussel by gripping the beard and pulling firmly towards the hinge. Discard any mussels with cracked shells, or open ones that do not close after being tapped sharply on the worktop.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large, lidded saucepan over a medium heat. Add {{shallot}} sliced shallots and cook for 3 minutes until softened. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 minute more. Pour in {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine and bring to a boil.

Tip the cleaned mussels into the pan. Clamp on the lid and cook over a high heat for 3 to 4 minutes, shaking the pan once or twice, until all the mussels have opened. Do not lift the lid in the first 2 minutes — the steam is what opens them.

Add {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley and toss. Discard any mussels that have not opened. Season the broth with black pepper — the mussels and butter will have provided enough salt. Ladle into deep bowls with plenty of the cooking liquid.

items.symptom: Many mussels remain closed after cooking
items.cause: Mussels that do not open are dead and should be discarded.
items.fix: A mussel that is closed after cooking was dead before it went in. Discard it. If most mussels are failing to open, the pan may not have been hot enough — the high heat and the lid together create the steam pressure needed.

items.cause: Too much liquid relative to the mussels, or the wine was low quality.
items.fix: The broth gets much of its flavour from the liquor the mussels release. Use the quantities given and don't be tempted to add more water. A poor-quality wine will produce a flat broth.

items.cause: Overcooked — left in the pot past the point they opened.
items.fix: As soon as the shells are open, the mussels are done. Serve immediately. Even a minute of additional heat toughens them significantly.

content.text: Moules marinières is the coastal cooking of Normandy and Brittany, where mussels are farmed on ropes along the Atlantic shore and white wine from the Loire is the natural accompaniment. The dish has been part of the bistro repertoire across France for well over a century. In Belgium it became moules-frites (mussels with chips) and crossed the Channel in that form. The Belgian and French versions are effectively the same dish; the debate over which country owns it has never been resolved and probably never will be.
